text: Psychological and lifestyle subjective health complaints are significantly associated with increased new injury risk within a 7-day lag period in endurance athletes.
population: endurance sport athletes (running, triathlon, swimming, cycling, rowing)

text: Sleeping less than 7 hours per day is associated with a 51% increased risk of new injury after a 14-day lag period in endurance athletes.
population: endurance sport athletes (running, triathlon, swimming, cycling, rowing)

text: Sleeping more than 7 hours per day is associated with a 37% decreased risk of new injury after a 14-day lag period in endurance athletes.
population: endurance sport athletes (running, triathlon, swimming, cycling, rowing)

text: There is a meaningful lag period (7-14 days) between reduced sleep quantity and its subsequent impact on injury risk, meaning the effects of sleep deprivation on injury are not immediate but cumulative.
population: endurance sport athletes (running, triathlon, swimming, cycling, rowing)

text: Subjective health complaints and sleep quantity are not significantly associated with training load factors, suggesting they represent independent risk pathways for injury beyond training load alone.
population: endurance sport athletes (running, triathlon, swimming, cycling, rowing)

text: Cardiorespiratory subjective health complaints showed a trend toward association with new injury risk but did not reach statistical significance.
population: endurance sport athletes (running, triathlon, swimming, cycling, rowing)
